Design philosophy

Design is not what it looks like. Design is how well it works and how fast people trust it.

When you design for operators in power plants, you learn that every pixel has a cost. I carry that standard into everything I build.

01

Clarity over cleverness

A beautiful interface that confuses the user has failed. Every decision starts with one question: does this help the user act with confidence?

02

Research is not optional

The problem you think exists is rarely the problem that matters. I talk to actual users — not personas, not assumptions — before designing anything.

03

Design closes the loop

I write HTML, CSS, and React. I review implementations. The gap between design intent and built reality is where most UX value is lost — I refuse to let that happen.

Core Design Work

Redesigning a 15-year-old legacy industrial platform to help operators confidently collaborate with AI in high-stakes environments.

The Human + AI Collaboration Model

The challenge wasn't prediction accuracy—it was trust. Operators needed clear visibility into what the AI detected, why it mattered, and how confident the system was in its recommendations before relying on it in high-stakes operational decisions.

Challenge 01 → Make AI actionable

The challenge wasn't prediction accuracy—it was trust. Operators needed clear visibility into Turn complex AI predictions into clear, actionable insights operators could quickly understand and act on..

Challenge 02 → Preserve Operator Muscle Memory

Modernize a 15-year-old platform while respecting operator muscle memory, maintaining familiar workflows, and building confidence in the new experience.

Challenge 03 → Build Trust in AI

Help operators understand, validate, and confidently act on AI recommendations in high-stakes environments.

MY DESIGN THINKING

The Human + AI
Collaboration Model

AI observing → AI informing → AI collaborating.

👁️
Mode 01 — Passive

Background Analyst

AI monitors continuously, surfaces anomalies passively. Operators gain awareness without distraction.

🔔
Mode 02 — Active

Alert System

Contextual alerts with confidence levels and supporting data. Engineered to cut alarm fatigue dramatically.

🤝
Mode 03 — Collaborative

Recommendation Engine

AI proposes actions with rationale. The operator reviews, approves, or overrides — always the final decision-maker.

MY DECISIONS

Three decisions that shaped the outcome

Decision 01

Contextual inquiry, not interviews

Interviews vs. Shadowing

Operators couldn't articulate workflow friction in a conference room — it only surfaced mid-shift, mid-incident. I chose 12 hours of embedded observation over scheduled interviews to capture decisions as they actually happened, not as remembered afterward.

Decision 02

4-layer information hierarchy over flat UI

Flat dashboard vs. Layered IA

The legacy system gave every data point equal visual weight. I restructured the IA into four urgency layers — critical, elevated, contextual, archival — so operators' eyes go where the risk is, not where the layout happens to put it.

Decision 03

Guardrails over open button logic

Free action vs. Constrained paths

AI recommendations could trigger irreversible batch actions. I designed confirmation gates and confidence thresholds directly into the interaction model — trust had to be earned per-action, not assumed system-wide.

Designing for AI Experiences

How trust gets built into a single decision

⚠
AI detects anomaly

Field research

12 Hours Inside the Control Room

Understanding operators before designing for them

12
Hours spent
observing operators
20+
Workflows documented
and analyzed
100+
Pain points captured
from real usage
3
Roles observed —
operators, supervisors, engineers
Critical alerts often missed — buried in low-contrast banners competing with routine notifications.
Information spread across screens — operators manually cross-referenced 3–4 monitors per decision.
High cognitive load during incidents — no visual triage between routine and urgent states.
Operators relied on experience over system guidance — the interface trailed behind their tacit knowledge.

Context & brief

ABB Ability System 800xA is used in power plants, oil & gas facilities, pharmaceutical manufacturing, and industrial environments globally. The platform had evolved over 15 years, accumulating design debt and an emerging need to integrate AI-driven insights without disrupting expert operator workflows.

My role

  • UX research & stakeholder interviews
  • Heuristic evaluation & cognitive load mapping
  • Human + AI Collaboration Model definition
  • User flows, wireframes & high-fidelity prototypes
  • Design system contribution (APUX & Arcadia)
  • Sprint facilitation & mentoring 6 junior designers
structure the own and proven design framework

Contextual inquiry in design framework

Traditional user interviews fail to capture the deep, subconscious instincts built by operators over 15+ years on a factory floor. By executing direct contextual inquiry inside the live control environment, I uncovered hidden edge cases and workflow gaps. This ensured our design interventions aligned perfectly with legacy muscle memory, minimizing operational disruption and training friction.

🎙️

UNDERSTAND

-> Contextual Field Research.

-> Legacy System & Tag Audit

🔬

EXPLORE

-> UX Strategy Definition

-> Smart Automation Vision & Guardrails

->Workflow Redesign

🧠

MATERIALIZED

-> Stress-Scenario Validation

->Scalable Design System

📊

AGILE METHOD

-> Sprint-based delivery cadence

->Continuous stakeholder feedback loops

ABB Ability System 800xA — POC Workflow Editor and industrial control interface

Design & delivery

  • Progressive disclosure — context-relevant information only
  • Contextual AI panels — inline, dismissible, confidence indicators
  • Redesigned alarm management — priority-ranked with history
  • Batch workflow simplification — 12-step process reduced to 5

Collaboration

  • Weekly design scrums with engineering & product
  • Bi-weekly usability reviews with domain experts
  • Specs, rationale & decision documentation
  • Reusable pattern library in APUX & Arcadia
  • Mentored 6 junior UX designers
Design system foundation

Principles that scaled across 260+ screens

01
Clarity under pressure

Every screen legible in under 3 seconds during high-stakes scenarios.

02
Consistency across workflows

One interaction grammar — operators never relearn a pattern mid-shift.

03
Accessibility for 24/7 ops

WCAG AA contrast minimums, built for fatigue and shift rotation.

04
Reduced cognitive load

Progressive disclosure — show only what the current task needs.

Component spec — Dialog

Dialogs needed two distinct presets: Overlay, for non-blocking information, and Dialog, for actions requiring explicit operator confirmation — critical for any AI-recommended action.

State variants (warning, error, success, info) use color and icon together, never color alone, to support colorblind operators and high-glare control room lighting.

Min width360px
Min height40px
Modal behaviorblocks underlying task
Non-modal behaviordraggable, stackable
Confirm batch override✕

AI recommends overriding temperature threshold on Line 4 based on 94% confidence pattern match. This action is logged and reversible.

The transformation

Same data. A completely different decision experience.

Reduced clicks4-layer hierarchy surfaces critical state without cross-referencing 4+ screens.
AI context inlineConfidence scores and reasoning appear next to the alert, not buried in a separate panel.
Guardrails visibleRecommended actions require explicit review — no silent automation, full audit trail.

"The goal wasn't to make the system look modern. It was to make the operator's decision in the next 30 seconds feel obvious — even when the data is anything but."

— Thomas Ravi Kiran
Outcomes & impact
25–35%
Faster operator decision-making
Validated through comparative usability testing. Operators reached correct decisions faster with lower error rates.
15 yrs
Legacy complexity resolved
Mapped and addressed 15 years of accumulated design debt from IA through interaction patterns.
87
Usability issues identified
Heuristic evaluation across 40+ screens — prioritised by severity, creating a clear design backlog.
3
AI interaction modes defined
Background Analyst, Alert System, and Recommendation Engine — each with distinct visual language.

What this taught me

Industrial UX demands a different muscle than consumer product design. The user is an expert. The consequences of a wrong decision can be catastrophic. The interface must amplify judgment, not replace it.

The biggest lesson: AI features fail not because the model is wrong, but because the interface doesn't give operators the confidence to act on it. Trust is a design problem.

What I believe

Three principles that shape every decision.

After 7+ years designing systems that real people depend on, these aren't abstract values — they're lessons earned in difficult projects.

01

Clarity over cleverness

A beautiful interface that confuses the user has failed. Every decision starts with: does this help the user act with confidence? If not, I simplify.

02

Design closes the loop

I write HTML, CSS, and React. I review implementations. The gap between design intent and built reality is where most UX value is lost.

03

Research is not optional

Every design that had real impact started with talking to actual users. Not personas. Not assumptions. The problem you think exists is rarely the real problem.
